content.text: Two of the most powerful structural principles in object-oriented design —

— determine how well a codebase tolerates change, guards against bugs, and scales with team size. In agentic AI systems, where multiple subsystems (LLM clients, memory stores, tool executors, budget trackers) interact continuously, these two principles are the difference between a maintainable architecture and a sprawling mess of global state.

is the bundling of data (attributes) with the methods that operate on that data, combined with the restriction of direct access to internal state from outside the class. The goal is to protect invariants — rules that must always be true about an object's internal data (for example, a token budget must never go negative). By routing all mutations through controlled methods, the class can enforce these invariants at every point of change.
In Python, strict access control through keywords does not exist the way it does in Java or C++. Instead, the language community uses naming conventions: a single leading underscore (
) signals "internal detail — please do not touch", and a double leading underscore (

) triggers name-mangling that makes the attribute harder (but not impossible) to access from outside the class. The practical effect is a social contract enforced by discipline rather than compiler errors.

hides the complexity of an implementation behind a clean, intention-revealing interface. Where encapsulation asks "who can touch this data?", abstraction asks "what can callers do with this object, and what details do they not need to see?" A well-designed abstract interface expresses operations in the language of the problem domain (
) rather than the language of the implementation (

The two principles reinforce each other: abstraction defines what the public API looks like, while encapsulation ensures that the private machinery behind that API cannot be bypassed. Together, they create a boundary between the code that "uses" a component and the code that "implements" it.

A typical agentic AI pipeline touches many external systems: LLM inference endpoints, embedding models, vector stores, relational databases, web scrapers, sandboxed code executors, and logging services. Without careful design, every caller must understand the internal layout of every subsystem. A single change to the vector store's API would ripple through every file that previously called its internals directly.

Encapsulation solves the mutation problem. Without it, any module can reach into an agent's message history and corrupt it, append a malformed turn, or silently clear it. With encapsulation, the message list lives behind a validated method. The invariant "messages must alternate user/assistant" can be enforced in one place rather than scattered across every caller.

Abstraction solves the knowledge problem. Without it, a developer writing an agent loop must understand HTTP retry logic, backoff strategies, token serialization, and JSON schema validation for the LLM provider. With abstraction, that developer calls
and trusts the implementation to handle the rest. When the provider changes its API, only the
class needs to change — not every agent that uses it.
state transitions are controlled by methods that can validate inputs, check preconditions, and reject illegal mutations before they corrupt internal data.
callers depend on the public interface, not the internals. Replace SQLite with Postgres, or swap OpenAI with Anthropic, by updating one class without touching any caller.
developers using a well-abstracted component need to understand its contract (method signatures and documented guarantees), not its implementation. A 500-line class presents a 5-method public API.
input validation, schema checks, and budget enforcement live in one place. New callers automatically get the same guarantees without copying logic.
encapsulated classes have clear boundaries. You can test the public API without depending on or mocking every internal detail. Private methods get exercised through the public interface.

because callers depend only on the public contract, internals can be rewritten freely — different data structures, different algorithms, different connection patterns — as long as the observed behavior stays the same.
developers must read a class's API documentation to discover valid operations. A simple dict lookup that previously took one line now requires understanding a method signature.
a leading underscore is a hint, not a wall. Determined or careless callers can access
and bypass all protections. The class must document its contract clearly and rely on team discipline for enforcement.

wrapping every operation in a class with a formal interface creates indirection that makes debugging harder. When a bug appears deep in a pipeline of abstract layers, the stack trace spans many files. The benefit of abstraction must outweigh the navigation cost.
property descriptors, validation setters, and factory methods add lines of code compared to plain dicts or dataclasses. For prototypes or scripts this overhead is often unjustified.
once a public API is in use, changing it breaks callers. A poorly designed abstraction "leaks" internal details into its interface (for example, a
method when the implementation detail of "which backend" should be invisible). Recovering from this requires versioning or a deprecation cycle.
Python uses three access patterns. Public names (no underscore) are part of the class's contract and may be used by any caller. Protected names (single underscore, e.g.,
) signal implementation detail; linters and IDE tooling typically warn when outside code accesses them. Mangled names (double underscore, e.g.,
) are rewritten by the interpreter to
, which prevents accidental access through inheritance but not through deliberate lookup.
decorator turns a method into an attribute access, allowing a class to compute a value, validate before setting, or raise an error on invalid state — all while presenting a clean
lets a class accept direct assignment syntax (
) while still validating the new value inside the setter.
A facade presents a simple, domain-oriented interface over a complex subsystem. The
example below hides the embedding step, the vector upsert, and the metadata lookup behind two high-level methods:
. Callers never interact with the vector index or metadata database directly; they use concepts from the problem domain ("remember this", "recall relevant items").

import hashlib

class MemoryStore:
    """Facade over a vector index and metadata store."""

    def __init__(self, vector_index, metadata_db, embedder) -> None:
        self._vector = vector_index
        self._meta = metadata_db
        self._embed = embedder

    def remember(self, text: str, tags: list[str] | None = None) -> str:
        """Store a text fragment and return its ID."""
        doc_id = hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
        vector = self._embed(text)
        self._vector.upsert(doc_id, vector)
        self._meta.insert(doc_id, text=text, tags=tags or [])
        return doc_id

    def recall(self, query: str, k: int = 5) -> list[str]:
        """Return the k most relevant stored texts."""
        query_vector = self._embed(query)
        ids = self._vector.search(query_vector, k=k)
        return [self._meta.get(doc_id)["text"] for doc_id in ids]

    def forget(self, doc_id: str) -> None:
        self._vector.delete(doc_id)
        self._meta.delete(doc_id)

The diagram below shows how encapsulation and abstraction create distinct layers in an agentic system. The agent loop at the top interacts only with the public interface (abstraction boundary). Behind that boundary, private state and private methods (encapsulation) handle all implementation details. External services are hidden entirely from the agent loop — it cannot reach them directly.

attrs.alt: Encapsulation and abstraction layers in an agentic AI architecture

content.text: The three internal compartments — private state, private methods, and validation guards — correspond to the three categories of implementation detail that encapsulation hides. None of these are visible or accessible to the agent loop. If the vector database is replaced, the internal compartments change; the public interface stays the same.
Mutable state must satisfy invariants (message ordering, budget limits, connection pool size).
Multiple callers might modify the same data concurrently or sequentially.
An object's internal representation is likely to change (switching from a list to a deque for message history) without the public API changing.
Sensitive values such as API keys or credentials live in the object and must not leak into logs.
The implementation is complex (HTTP retries, embedding pipelines, connection management) but callers need only a simple "send and receive" contract.
You want to swap implementations without touching callers — different LLM providers, different vector databases, local vs. cloud storage.
A subsystem has multiple callers that would otherwise duplicate the same setup and configuration code.
For one-off scripts, notebooks, or throw-away experiments where the overhead of class design slows iteration without adding value.
When the data has no invariants to protect and no complex operations — a simple
When the abstraction leaks — if callers must pass internal implementation details as parameters, the abstraction is not hiding enough and adds complexity without benefit.
The following step-by-step example builds a
class that encapsulates message history with a token budget and exposes a clean interface to the agent loop.
Before writing a class, list the rules that must always hold. For a token-limited session:
Messages must alternate roles: user → assistant → user → …
Total token count must never exceed the configured limit.
Once the session is closed, no new messages may be added.
Choose method names that reflect what callers need, not how the class works internally. Callers want to
the session. They do not want to know about the internal list, the token counter, or the ordering check.
This minimal example shows how a property setter prevents invalid state from being written, using a temperature parameter as the demonstration. It is self-contained and can be run directly.
Example 2 — abstracted LLM client with retry (realistic)
This example shows an abstracted LLM client that hides exponential-backoff retry logic, header construction, and response parsing behind a single
method. The agent loop is insulated from all HTTP details.

Production agentic systems like LangChain, LlamaIndex, and the Anthropic Agents SDK each use encapsulation and abstraction extensively. Consider a multi-step research agent that must browse the web, retrieve documents, summarize them, and return a structured answer. Each subsystem presents an abstract interface:
— hides pagination, rate limiting, and HTML parsing
— hides PDF parsing, chunking, and embedding
The orchestrator loop that wires these together is under 30 lines precisely because it knows nothing about the internals of each subsystem:

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ResearchResult:
    question: str
    answer: str
    sources: list[str]


class ResearchOrchestrator:
    """Orchestrates multi-step research using abstract subsystem interfaces."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        search: WebSearchTool,
        store: DocumentStore,
        summarizer: SummaryAgent,
        max_sources: int = 5,
    ) -> None:
        self._search = search
        self._store = store
        self._summarizer = summarizer
        self._max_sources = max_sources
        self._run_count = 0

    def research(self, question: str) -> ResearchResult:
        """Run a full research cycle and return a structured answer."""
        self._run_count += 1

        # 1. Search for relevant pages
        results = self._search.run(question)[:self._max_sources]

        # 2. Ingest each URL into the document store
        doc_ids = [self._store.ingest(r.url) for r in results]

        # 3. Summarize each document relative to the question
        summaries = [self._summarizer.summarize(doc_id, question) for doc_id in doc_ids]

        # 4. Combine summaries into final answer
        combined = "\n\n".join(summaries)
        final_answer = self._summarizer.synthesize(question, combined)

        return ResearchResult(
            question=question,
            answer=final_answer,
            sources=[r.url for r in results],
        )

    @property
    def run_count(self) -> int:
        return self._run_count

has zero knowledge of HTTP, PDF parsers, vector indexes, or LLM APIs. Swapping the search engine from DuckDuckGo to Google, or replacing the vector store, requires changing only the concrete class passed to the constructor — not the orchestrator itself. This is the practical payoff of abstraction at scale.
Returning a direct reference to a private mutable container allows callers to bypass all validation:
Fake encapsulation — public attributes with comments

A property setter without validation gives callers false confidence that the assignment is safe. If the setter simply stores the value without checking, it provides no more protection than a plain attribute — but adds indirection overhead.
write how you want callers to use the class before writing any implementation. This avoids the trap of exposing internals because they were convenient to implement.
this single convention, applied consistently, makes the boundary visible in every editor and code review.
when exposing collections that callers should not modify.
all validation belongs in the mutating methods (setters,
methods, constructors). Internal private methods can trust that their input is already valid.
for attribute control on performance-critical classes:
this prevents dynamic attribute creation and clarifies which attributes a class owns.
write them in the class docstring. Readers should not have to infer that "remaining_tokens is always >= 0" from reading every setter.

not every attribute needs a property. Simple value objects with no invariants are cleaner as dataclasses. Properties have a runtime cost and a readability cost; use them only when access must be controlled or computed.
raises if the budget is exceeded. The object knows its own state best.

Encapsulation and abstraction have minimal runtime overhead in Python. Property access involves a descriptor lookup (roughly the same cost as a method call) instead of a direct attribute access. In benchmarks on CPython 3.12, the difference between reading a plain attribute and reading a property is roughly 50–100 nanoseconds per access — negligible in any application that performs I/O (LLM calls, database queries) in the critical path.
The primary performance consideration is not the property overhead but the
and the message history is very long (thousands of messages), each call allocates a new tuple. For this pattern, consider returning a
or using a cursor/pagination API instead of snapshotting the full history.
abstract interfaces enable dependency injection. This is the foundation of horizontal scaling — a
backed by Redis can replace one backed by an in-process dict without any changes to the agent code.

abstracted interfaces are trivially mockable. Integration tests run fast because the LLM client, vector store, and HTTP layer can each be replaced with in-memory fakes that implement the same interface.
without changing the public API. Callers are unaffected.
when the return type is set but the value is not writable, and enforce return-type consistency.
) when code outside a class reads
: catches common mistakes in property definitions and mutable default arguments.
Runtime enforcement with dataclasses and attrs

from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable


@runtime_checkable
class VectorStore(Protocol):
    def upsert(self, doc_id: str, vector: list[float]) -> None: ...
    def search(self, query: list[float], k: int) -> list[str]: ...
    def delete(self, doc_id: str) -> None: ...


# Any class with these methods satisfies the protocol — no inheritance required:
class ChromaVectorStore:
    def __init__(self, collection_name: str) -> None: ...
    def upsert(self, doc_id: str, vector: list[float]) -> None: ...
    def search(self, query: list[float], k: int) -> list[str]: ...
    def delete(self, doc_id: str) -> None: ...


def build_memory(store: VectorStore) -> "MemoryStore":
    # Works with any class satisfying VectorStore — no import of the concrete type
    ...
Several Python patterns can be used to structure data and behavior. The table below compares encapsulated classes with alternatives, highlighting when each is appropriate:

Encapsulation is the correct mechanism for protecting API keys and tokens inside long-running agent processes. By storing them in double-underscore attributes and never exposing them through any property or repr, they cannot be accidentally logged, serialized, or accessed from outside the class:

When tool arguments arrive from an LLM's tool-call response, they are user-derived data and must be validated before use. Encapsulating the tool executor ensures that validation cannot be bypassed — there is one path to execution, and it always runs the schema check:

Reliability in agentic systems often fails at state corruption: a retry sends the same user turn twice, producing a malformed history; a race condition increments the budget twice. Encapsulation makes these bugs detectable. When
enforces role ordering, a duplicate user turn raises an exception immediately rather than producing a corrupt history that causes a mysterious failure ten steps later.
Think of a well-designed class as a

. Customers interact with tellers at the counter (the public interface). The tellers accept deposits and withdrawals (method calls), validate identification (input validation), enforce transaction limits (invariant enforcement), and return receipts (return values). The vault, the ledger, the reserve calculations, and the interbank settlement protocols are completely invisible to customers (encapsulation). Different branches may use different vault manufacturers or ledger systems — customers see the same counter (abstraction).

A common beginner mistake is to think of encapsulation as "hiding things for security". The more useful frame is "hiding things to reduce the surface of things that can change". Every public attribute is a promise: "I guarantee this will always be here, with this type, with this name." The fewer promises made, the more freely the internals can evolve.
Encapsulation bundles data with the methods that mutate it and enforces invariants by controlling access — in Python via
Abstraction hides implementation complexity behind a domain-oriented public interface so callers never depend on details that might change.
Together they create a stable boundary between "what a component does" and "how it does it", enabling internals to be refactored without breaking callers.
) from properties to prevent callers from bypassing validation by mutating internal containers.
Validate all inputs at the public API boundary — mutating methods and property setters — so no caller path can bypass invariant enforcement.
classes for structural abstraction in Python: any class with the required methods satisfies the protocol without forced inheritance hierarchies.
The main cost of abstraction is interface rigidity: once the public API is in use, changing it requires versioning. Design the interface to express domain concepts, not implementation details.
Sensitive values (API keys, tokens) belong in double-underscore attributes with repr methods that redact them — never in public attributes or log output.
Over-abstraction is a real risk: wrap only what genuinely benefits from a stable interface. Plain dataclasses or TypedDicts are more appropriate when there are no invariants to protect.
